core_identity.title: Symmetry & Structure: A Lesson Inspired by Emmy Noether
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Identify what stays the same (invariants) and what changes under reflections, rotations, translations and enlargements, and use this idea to reason about symmetry and structure.

verse_ality.driving_question: What remains invariant when we transform a shape, and why does that idea matter for understanding symmetry and structure?

knowing_world.focus: Use the four core transformations to test what changes and what stays the same, building the habit of naming invariants (e.g., lengths, angles, orientation, scale).

human_story.focus: Learn about Emmy Noether and how her work connected symmetry to deep structure in mathematics and physics, while reflecting on justice and gender equality in education.
justice_prompts: Why is it important to learn about mathematicians like Emmy Noether?
How do her struggles and achievements relate to gender equality in education and careers today?

expressive_self.focus: Play creatively with invariants and symmetry by inventing characters, diagrams, and stories that highlight what changes and what stays the same.
activities: Invent a character called “Invariant” — describe their power and weakness.
Draw a diagram showing what changes and what doesn’t in one transformation.
Write a short story where a character is stuck in a loop of symmetry.

designed_world.focus: Spot symmetry in society and design, and apply transformations to create or analyse patterns, logos, and systems where structure and consistency matter (including coding and encryption).
design_challenges: Take a photo of something symmetrical in your environment and identify which transformations map it onto itself.
Design a logo or pattern using at least two transformations (e.g., reflect then rotate).
Research how symmetry and algebra are used in encryption, coding, or game design and summarise one example.

regulation_supports: Mirror a friend’s movement exactly (hand gestures, posture, steps), then reverse roles and notice what stayed the same or changed.
wellbeing_prompts: When do you feel most balanced or symmetrical in your thinking or routine?

pathways.name: Poster: What Remains?
pathways.focus: Create a poster showing the four transformations and clearly state what remains invariant (and what changes) in each.
products: At least one worked example for each transformation (reflection, rotation, translation, enlargement).
A short invariant list for each transformation (e.g., lengths, angles, orientation, scale).
One paragraph linking invariants to the idea of symmetry.
criteria: Conceptual Understanding – Shows what is invariant and why
Application – Connects invariants to symmetry in real systems

pathways.name: Creative Reflection: Fairness and Consistency
pathways.focus: Write a creative reflection responding to: “What does symmetry teach us about fairness or consistency?”
products: A creative response (story, poem, spoken reflection, or illustrated writing).
At least two mathematical examples of invariants that support your reflection.
A short note on Emmy Noether and why her story matters.

pathways.name: Design a Code or Puzzle
pathways.focus: Design a code, game mechanic, or puzzle based on modular arithmetic and symmetry-inspired structure.
products: A puzzle or code mechanic with rules/instructions.
A short explanation of the symmetry/structure or invariants involved (e.g., modular patterns, repeated transformations).
Optional: test it on a classmate and improve it based on feedback.
criteria: Fluency & Accuracy – Correct logic and mathematical structure
Application – Links maths to coding/game design
Conceptual Understanding – Identifies invariants or preserved structure

required: Basic understanding of 2D shapes and coordinates
Simple angle facts and length measurement ideas
Comfort describing and naming transformations at an intuitive level

builds_toward: Links between symmetry and conservation laws (Noether’s Theorem, as enrichment)
